claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

- Is your child **under 16 years old**?
- Is your child **between 16 and 19 years old**?
- Is your child **20 years old or over**?

Please let me know the age of the child (or children) you're claiming for, and I'll guide you through the next steps.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi there! Yes, I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my two children. I have Alex who's 17, and Blake who's just turned 18. They both live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're looking to claim Child Benefit for:
- **Alex**, aged 17
- **Blake**, aged 18

Since both children are between 16 and 19 years old, I'll need to check if they meet the education or training requirements. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their education or training status.

**For each child, I need to know:**

1. **Are they currently in approved education or training?** (This includes A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications)

2. **Have they recently left education or training?** (Within the last few months)

3. **Or are they neither in education/training nor recently left?**

Could you tell me:
- **Alex (17)**: Is Alex currently in education or training? If so, what type?
- **Blake (18)**: Is Blake currently in education or training? If so, what type?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Sure! 

Alex is still in sixth form doing his A-levels, so yes, he's in education.

Blake actually finished his studies and isn't in any education or training at the moment - he's just been working and trying to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that!

Let me assess each child separately:

---

**For Alex (17) - in sixth form doing A-levels:**

Let me check if A-levels meet the education requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news for Alex! **A-levels are on the approved qualifications list.** 

I just need to confirm a couple more things about Alex's education:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/lessons)
2. **Is the course funded by an employer** or linked to a guaranteed job with an employer afterwards?

Assuming sixth form A-levels are standard full-time and not employer-funded, Alex should qualify - but please confirm!

---

**For Blake (18) - finished studies and now working:**

You mentioned Blake finished his studies and isn't in education or training. 

**When did Blake finish his education?** This is important because there may be an "extension period" where Child Benefit can continue for a short time after leaving education (up to 20 weeks in some cases), depending on when he left.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - yes, he's doing full-time A-levels at sixth form, the usual setup with classes and coursework throughout the week. No, it's not employer-funded or anything like that, just regular sixth form.

For Blake - he finished school back in the summer, so that was several months ago now. He's been out of education since then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details!

---

**For Alex (17) - Full-time A-levels, not employer-funded:**

This sounds like approved education. Let me just check one more requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:** Does Alex work **less than 24 hours per week** in paid employment (or not work at all)?

This is important because children aged 16+ who work 24 or more hours per week may not be eligible.

---

**For Blake (18) - left education in the summer:**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Blake finished school in the summer (several months ago), that would be more than 20 weeks ago. Unfortunately, the extension period only covers up to **20 weeks** after leaving education, and Blake would need to have registered with certain services during that time.

Based on what you've told me, Blake no longer qualifies for Child Benefit since he:
- Is not in approved education or training
- Left education more than 20 weeks ago
- Is currently working

---

**Let me continue with Alex's assessment.** Does Alex work at all, and if so, is it **less than 24 hours per week**?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - Alex is fully focused on his studies, so he doesn't work at all. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

Is Alex **NOT receiving any benefits in his own right**, such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

In other words, is Alex free from claiming these benefits himself? (Most students in sixth form wouldn't be claiming these, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex is doing A-levels at sixth form (not an apprenticeship), that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent progress! Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following residency requirements?**

1. **You normally live in the UK** (most common)
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working/looking for work/studying
4. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK. **Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great - living in Manchester, you meet the UK residency requirement.

**Next, I need to confirm you are responsible for Alex.**

You mentioned earlier that both children live at home with you. Does Alex live with you?

(If for some reason a child doesn't live with you, you can still qualify if you contribute to their upkeep - such as paying for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Since Alex lives with you, that satisfies the responsibility requirement.

**One more check:** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex? 

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- An ex-partner
- A grandparent or other family member

If no one else is claiming (or if there's an agreement that you should be the one to claim), we can proceed.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**About Alex's living situation:**

Is Alex currently:
- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)?
- **In local authority care**?
- **In a fostering arrangement**?
- **Or living normally at home with you** (the usual situation)?

I assume since Alex is at home doing sixth form, it's the normal living situation - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex is just living normally at home with me, nothing unusual like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, you're supporting Alex yourself without the council paying for his housing or upkeep. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. We're supporting him ourselves.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! I've now completed the assessment for both children. Let me provide you with the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Child Benefit Eligibility Results

### ✅ Alex (17) — **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You **can claim Child Benefit for Alex**. He meets all the requirements:
- He's 17 and in approved full-time education (A-levels)
- His education is not employer-funded
- He doesn't work
- He doesn't claim benefits in his own name
- He lives with you at home
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council isn't paying for his upkeep

### ❌ Blake (18) — **NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you **cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake**. This is because:
- Although he's under 20, he's no longer in approved education or training
- He left education in the summer, which is more than 20 weeks ago (the maximum extension period)
- He is currently working rather than studying

---

## 📝 Next Steps for Alex

To claim Child Benefit for Alex:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. Payments are made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### ⚠️ Important Note
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may be subject to the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through tax. You can choose to receive the payments and pay the charge, or opt out of payments while still protecting your National Insurance record.

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved education (full-time A-levels at sixth form). 2) The education is non-advanced and not employer-funded. 3) He does not work (well under the 24-hour limit). 4) He does not receive benefits in his own right. 5) He is not in an apprenticeship. 6) The claimant lives in the UK and normally resides with Alex. 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. 8) He is living at home normally (not in hospital, care, or fostering). 9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19 but is not in approved education or training. 2) He left education in the summer (several months ago), which exceeds the 20-week extension period. 3) He is currently working and not pursuing further education or training. Therefore, he no longer qualifies as a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for one of their two children. Alex (17) qualifies as he meets all requirements: he's in approved full-time education (A-levels), doesn't work, doesn't claim benefits, lives at home with the claimant in Manchester, and no one else is claiming for him. Blake (18) does not qualify because he left education several months ago (exceeding the 20-week extension period) and is not in any approved education or training.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: 1) He is aged 16-19 but is not in approved education or training. 2) He left education in the summer (several months ago), which exceeds the 20-week extension period. 3) He is currently working and not pursuing further education or training. Therefore, he no longer qualifies as a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for one of their two children. Alex (17) qualifies as he meets all requirements: he's in approved full-time education (A-levels), doesn't work, doesn't claim benefits, lives at home with the claimant in Manchester, and no one else is claiming for him. Blake (18) does not qualify because he left education several months ago (exceeding the 20-week extension period) and is not in any approved education or training.
